{"agent":"reviewer_1","payload":{"summary":"The paper presents a novel approach to imitation learning by integrating causal inference into inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). The authors propose new graphical conditions that help in learning effective policies even in the presence of unobserved confounders (UCs) and when the state-action spaces of the imitator and expert differ. The framework is designed to leverage existing IRL algorithms, such as MWAL and GAIL, in environments with UCs. The approach is validated through simulations using both real-world and synthetic data.","significance_and_novelty":"The paper introduces a significant advancement by addressing the challenge of unobserved confounders in IRL, a problem that has not been thoroughly explored in the literature. The use of causal graphical models to derive conditions under which imitation learning can be successful is novel and could,","reasons_for_acceptance":["The paper addresses a significant gap in the literature by tackling the problem of unobserved confounders in IRL.","The proposed graphical conditions and causal formulation are novel and provide a robust framework for imitation learning.","The approach is theoretically sound, with rigorous proofs and derivations provided.","The framework is extensible and compatible with existing IRL algorithms, enhancing its practical applicability."],"reasons_for_rejection":["The paper could benefit from more extensive empirical validation to demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed method.","Some assumptions made in the theoretical analysis, such as the availability of parametric knowledge about the reward function, may limit the applicability of the approach in real-world scenarios."],"suggestions":["Provide more detailed empirical results, including comparisons with baseline methods, to strengthen the validation of the proposed approach.","Discuss the limitations and potential real-world applicability of the assumptions made in the theoretical framework, such as the availability of parametric knowledge about the reward function.","Consider exploring the impact of different types of unobserved confounders on the effectiveness of the proposed method."],"rating":8,"confidence":4},"input_message":"Analyze the paper and provide a structured review.","context_used":"Paper source: 3199_causal_imitation_learning_via.pdf

ABSTRACT
One of the most common ways children learn when unfamiliar with the environment
is by mimicking adults. Imitation learning concerns an imitator learning to behave
in an unknown environment from an expert’s demonstration; reward signals remain
latent to the imitator. This paper studies imitation learning through causal lenses
and extends the analysis and tools developed for behavior cloning (Zhang, Kumor,
Bareinboim, 2020) to inverse reinforcement learning. First, we propose novel
graphical conditions that allow the imitator to learn a policy performing as well as
the expert’s behavior policy, even when the imitator and the expert’s state-action
space disagree, and unobserved confounders (UCs) are present. When provided
with parametric knowledge about the unknown reward function, s

one to leverage existing IRL algorithms even when UCs are present, including
the multiplicative-weights algorithm (MWAL) (Syed & Schapire, 2008) and the
generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) (Ho & Ermon, 2016). Finally, we
validate our framework by simulations using real-world and synthetic data.

meta_review.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to imitation learning by integrating causal inference into inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). It addresses the challenge of unobserved confounders (UCs) and discrepancies in state-action spaces between the expert and imitator, which are significant issues in the field. The proposed framework introduces new graphical conditions and leverages existing IRL algorithms, with validation through simulations using both real-world and synthetic data. All reviewers agree on the novelty and theoretical soundness of the approach, highlighting its potential impact on,
key_points: The paper introduces a novel causal framework for IRL that effectively addresses the issue of unobserved confounders, a significant challenge in imitation learning.
The approach is theoretically sound, with rigorous proofs and derivations, and is compatible with existing IRL algorithms, enhancing its practical applicability.
The framework is validated through simulations, showing improved performance over traditional methods, although more extensive empirical validation is suggested by reviewers.
Reviewers suggest that the paper could benefit from more comprehensive comparisons with a wider range of baselines and a detailed discussion on computational complexity.
Some assumptions, such as the availability of parametric knowledge about the reward function, may limit the applicability of the approach in real-world scenarios.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to imitation learning by integrating causal inference into inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). The authors propose new graphical conditions that help in learning effective policies even in the presence of unobserved confounders (UCs) and when the state-action spaces of the imitator and expert differ. The framework is designed to leverage existing IRL algorithms, such as MWAL and GAIL, in environments with UCs. The approach is validated through simulations using both real-world and synthetic data.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper introduces a significant advancement by addressing the challenge of unobserved confounders in IRL, a problem that has not been thoroughly explored in the literature. The use of causal graphical models to derive conditions under which imitation learning can be successful is novel and could,
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper addresses a significant gap in the literature by tackling the problem of unobserved confounders in IRL.
The proposed graphical conditions and causal formulation are novel and provide a robust framework for imitation learning.
The approach is theoretically sound, with rigorous proofs and derivations provided.
The framework is extensible and compatible with existing IRL algorithms, enhancing its practical applicability.
reasons_for_rejection: The paper could benefit from more extensive empirical validation to demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed method.
Some assumptions made in the theoretical analysis, such as the availability of parametric knowledge about the reward function, may limit the applicability of the approach in real-world scenarios.
suggestions: Provide more detailed empirical results, including comparisons with baseline methods, to strengthen the validation of the proposed approach.
Discuss the limitations and potential real-world applicability of the assumptions made in the theoretical framework, such as the availability of parametric knowledge about the reward function.
Consider exploring the impact of different types of unobserved confounders on the effectiveness of the proposed method.

response_payload.summary: The paper introduces a novel approach to imitation learning by applying causal inference techniques to inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). It proposes graphical conditions that enable learning effective policies in the presence of unobserved confounders (UCs) and state-action space discrepancies between the expert and the imitator. The framework is validated through simulations on both real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating improved performance over traditional methods.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper presents a significant advancement in the field of imitation learning by integrating causal inference into IRL. This approach addresses the challenge of unobserved confounders, which is a common issue in real-world scenarios but often overlooked in existing methods. The novelty lies in the
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper introduces a novel causal framework for IRL that addresses unobserved confounders, a significant challenge in imitation learning.
The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in experiments compared to traditional BC and IRL methods.
The approach is theoretically grounded with clear graphical conditions and is extensible to existing IRL algorithms.
reasons_for_rejection: The experimental section could benefit from more comprehensive comparisons with a wider range of baselines.
The paper lacks detailed discussion on the computational complexity of the proposed method.
suggestions: Include more diverse baselines in the experimental evaluation to strengthen the empirical validation.
Provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity and scalability of the proposed framework.
Consider additional real-world scenarios to further demonstrate the applicability and robustness of the method.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) by incorporating causal inference techniques to address the challenge of unobserved confounders (UCs) in expert demonstrations. It introduces a causal IRL framework that uses structural causal models (SCMs) and provides a graphical criterion for determining when an imitating policy can be effectively learned, even in the presence of UCs. The approach is robust against confounding bias and extends existing IRL algorithms to non-Markovian settings.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper presents a significant advancement in the field of imitation learning by addressing the often overlooked issue of unobserved confounders in IRL. By introducing a causal framework, it allows for a more robust learning of policies that can perform at least as well as the expert, even when Uc
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper addresses a critical issue in IRL, namely the presence of unobserved confounders, which can significantly impact the performance of learned policies.
The introduction of a causal framework and graphical criteria is a novel contribution that enhances the robustness of IRL methods.
The work extends existing IRL algorithms to non-Markovian settings, broadening their applicability.
reasons_for_rejection: The paper could benefit from more extensive empirical validation across diverse domains to demonstrate the generality of the proposed approach.
Some technical aspects, such as the implementation details of the causal IRL framework, could be more thoroughly explained.
suggestions: Include more empirical results to validate the proposed approach in different environments, highlighting its robustness against unobserved confounders.
Provide detailed explanations and examples of the graphical criteria and how they are applied in practice.
Clarify the implementation details of the causal IRL framework to aid in reproducibility.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
One of the most common ways children learn when unfamiliar with the environment
is by mimicking adults. Imitation learning concerns an imitator learning to behave
in an unknown environment from an expert’s demonstration; reward signals remain
latent to the imitator. This paper studies imitation learning through causal lenses
and extends the analysis and tools developed for behavior cloning (Zhang, Kumor,
Bareinboim, 2020) to inverse reinforcement learning. First, we propose novel
graphical conditions that allow the imitator to learn a policy performing as well as
the expert’s behavior policy, even when the imitator and the expert’s state-action
space disagree, and unobserved confounders (UCs) are present. When provided
with parametric knowledge about the unknown reward function, s tate-action
space disagree, and unobserved confounders (UCs) are present. When provided
with parametric knowledge about the unknown reward function, such a policy
may outperform the expert’s. Also, our method is easily extensible and allows
one to leverage existing IRL algorithms even when UCs are present, including
the multiplicative-weights algorithm (MWAL) (Syed & Schapire, 2008) and the
generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) (Ho & Ermon, 2016). Finally, we
validate our framework by simulations using real-world and synthetic data.
